title: Access Control for Vector Stores
concept: Authentication, per-collection permissions, and audit logging for a production vector database.

exercises.prompt: When versioning vector collections during a database migration, why do we run the old and new indexes side-by-side?

options.text: To double the speed of searches by querying both collections at once.
To test the new index without downtime or quality regressions.
To bypass the need for any database access controls during updates.

exercises.explain: Running the old and new collections side-by-side allows you to verify the performance and accuracy of the new index before safely routing all traffic and retiring the old index.

cards.title: The Hidden Risk of Embeddings
cards.body: Vector databases contain dense mathematical representations of your proprietary data, code, or user chats. Without proper security controls, a single leaked API key could expose all of your organization's semantic knowledge.

cards.title: Securing Vector Stores
cards.body: Access control in vector stores ensures that users and applications can only search or modify the specific embedding collections they are authorized to touch. It relies on verifying identity (authentication), limiting access scopes (permissions), and keeping history (audit logs).

cards.title: The High-Security Bank Vault
cards.body: Think of a vector database like a high-security bank vault. Instead of letting anyone browse all safety deposit boxes, the bank checks your ID (Authentication), only gives you a key to your specific box (Per-Collection Permissions), and keeps a CCTV record of every time a box is opened (Audit Logging).

cards.title: The Chatbot Guardrails
cards.body: A customer support chatbot is authenticated using a limited token. The vector database restricts its permissions to read-only on the 'public_faqs' collection, preventing it from writing new vectors or viewing the 'billing_details' collection.

cards.title: Myth: Vectors Are Already Encrypted
cards.body: Because embeddings look like random lists of numbers, some believe they are safe if intercepted. However, modern models can reconstruct the original sensitive text or images directly from these vectors. Access control is mandatory!

cards.title: Open vs. Secured Vector Stores
cards.body: Compare the risk of open access versus a secured vector database environment.

left.text: Any API key can read/write any collection; no record of who queried what.

right.text: Per-collection tokens restrict access; query logs track every search.

exercises.prompt: What is the benefit of using per-collection permissions in a vector database?

options.text: It speeds up the generation of embeddings on the client side.
It restricts a client's access to only the specific collections they need.
It automatically duplicates the index for backup purposes.
It translates high-dimensional vectors into smaller key-value pairs.

exercises.explain: Per-collection permissions ensure that an identity (like a chatbot) can only access the specific set of vectors it needs to do its job, preventing wider data leaks.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Once a client is successfully authenticated, they should automatically have full read and write access to all collections in the vector database.
exercises.explain: Authentication verifies who the user or application is, but permissions (authorization) determine what they are allowed to do once inside.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the security practice of recording who searched the vector database.
exercises.sentence: To track down unauthorized searches, we must enable audit logging on our vector database.
exercises.explain: Audit logging is the practice of recording database operations—like who queried which collection—to detect and investigate potential security breaches.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes an AI or automated agent monitoring vector database security?

options.text: An automated security agent parses the vector database's audit logs, flagging an IP address that made 50,000 semantic searches in under two minutes.
An engineer manually writes an SQL script to delete duplicate vectors from a collection during off-peak hours.
A developer runs a python script to change the distance metric of a collection from Euclidean to Cosine.

exercises.explain: The first option represents an AI system systematically reviewing the security logs (audit logs) to identify anomalous query volume, which indicates a potential data scraping attempt.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a secure vector search service for a hospital's patient records. How should you configure the vector database to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive medical embeddings while maintaining compliance?

options.text: Implement API keys, allow global read access, and rely on client-side application logs.
Implement role-based access control, but disable audit logging to maximize search speed.
Require token authentication, restrict the client's role to read-only on that specific collection, and enable query audit logging.
Encrypt the vectors on the client side, bypassing database-level access controls entirely.

exercises.explain: To fully secure a production vector database, you need to verify identity (Authentication), restrict access to the specific collection (Per-Collection Permissions), and write a record of the search query to a secure log file (Audit Logging) to detect leaks or abuse.
